caveats: a: No labor-compensation ratio is available in the frozen dataset, so aggregate labor opportunity remains unevidenced.
a: Production phase, barn age, ownership of pigs, and outsourced veterinary or feed services materially change the task mix.
rho: Research demonstrations are not industry-wide adoption evidence.
rho: Integrators may own data and prescribe technology, leaving contract growers little implementation discretion.
e: The frozen dataset has no defensible LMM firm count.
e: Farm counts include small or diversified operations, while inventory and revenue are highly concentrated among large producers.
s5: National producer age is not hog-specific and does not measure completed transfers.
s5: Multiple family producers and corporate or integrator ownership weaken the age-to-control bridge.
q: No contract sample directly measures automation pass-through.
q: Independent ownership, integrator-owned pigs, and specialized phase contracts allocate costs and benefits differently.
d5: Pork output is downstream of farms and can grow through productivity rather than more farm-service quantity.
d5: Industry growth may concentrate in large integrated operations outside the LMM lens.
o: The accountable operator may consolidate across sites or shift from grower to integrator.
o: Cultivated-meat substitution belongs primarily in demand rather than operator requirement and is not double-counted here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat livestock revenue through formula or negotiated hog sales, marketing contracts, packer programs, and production contracts that pay growers for housing and caring for pigs owned by an integrator, with economics affected by feed, productivity, mortality, weight, quality, facility terms, and environmental compliance.
lens.excluded_activities: Hobby and subsistence farms, pork processors without farming, livestock transport and stockyards, captive internal facilities without external revenue, genetics or veterinary businesses outside farm production, shells, and non-control land or financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market commercial hog and pig farms with recurring external revenue from breeding, farrowing, nursery, feeder-pig, finishing, or farrow-to-finish production sold to integrators, processors, livestock buyers, or other farms, including independent and production-contract growers.

a.bridge: USDA precision-swine projects demonstrate monitorable activities such as detection, tracking, posture, identity, body condition, feeding interactions, farrowing, and distress, but do not measure wage-weighted AI exposure in commercial farms. I map continuous observation, anomaly triage, feed and environment optimization, breeding and health records, inventory and weight forecasting, scheduling, compliance, purchasing, contracting, and administration into exposed wages; research prototypes may overstate current commercial readiness while omitting physical care and maintenance.
caveats: No labor-compensation ratio is available in the frozen dataset, so aggregate labor opportunity remains unevidenced.
Production phase, barn age, ownership of pigs, and outsourced veterinary or feed services materially change the task mix.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across farrow-to-wean, nursery, finishing, and farrow-to-finish farms, separating owner, caretaker, veterinary, maintenance, feed, manure, compliance, contract, and administrative work and documenting existing controllers, sensors, robotic feeding, and farm software.
a.rationale: Confined production produces dense sensor, feed, health, reproduction, environmental, and performance data suitable for prediction and targeted intervention. Animal handling, treatment, breeding assistance, loading, cleaning, repairs, manure work, biosecurity, and emergency response remain physical and accountable.

d5.bridge: USDA forecasts pork production and exports, not a five-year constant-price quantity index for eligible LMM hog farms. I translate near-term production growth and projected export expansion through 2035 into moderate growth, then widen downward for consolidation, productivity per sow, disease, feed costs, trade shocks, environmental restrictions, and the possibility that volume accrues to large integrators outside the lens.
caveats: Pork output is downstream of farms and can grow through productivity rather than more farm-service quantity.
Industry growth may concentrate in large integrated operations outside the LMM lens.
d5.change_evidence: USDA long-term pork production, consumption, export, slaughter-weight, litter-size, and inventory series; packer capacity and plant closures; feed-price forecasts; contract placements; disease surveillance; and state permitting and animal-welfare implementation.
d5.rationale: Domestic pork availability is stable and exports are projected to grow, supporting hog output. Higher weights and pigs per litter can meet demand with fewer breeding animals or farms, while African swine fever, trade disputes, feed volatility, processing capacity, state animal-welfare rules, and environmental opposition create downside.

caveats: The frozen dataset has no defensible LMM firm count.
Farm counts include small or diversified operations, while inventory and revenue are highly concentrated among large producers.
e.change_evidence: Map Census of Agriculture and NASS operations to integrator ownership, contract status, annual hog sales, capacity, production phase, legal entity, employees, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, barn age, debt, permits, manure plans, land tenure, and owner dependency.
e.rationale: Commercial specialized farms and contract growers provide repeat external output, but eligibility is reduced by small diversified farms, integrator-owned facilities, large consolidated operators, thin contract-grower economics, owner labor, single-integrator concentration, aging barns, environmental liabilities, and firms outside normalized LMM earnings. The missing firm denominator prevents target quantity estimation.

l.detail: SUSB scope excludes this activity, no IPS labor-compensation/output series at any level, and no other receipts source downloaded; labor_share left null (recorded gap).
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: The accountable operator may consolidate across sites or shift from grower to integrator.
Cultivated-meat substitution belongs primarily in demand rather than operator requirement and is not double-counted here.
o.change_evidence: Commercial autonomous-barn approvals, remote multi-site management, integrator insourcing, robotics adoption, animal-welfare and veterinary rules, insurance requirements, and evidence on contract growers being replaced by fully integrator-operated facilities.
o.rationale: Producing live hogs requires an accountable operator for animal welfare, feeding, environment, treatment, biosecurity, manure, facilities, records, and emergency response. Sensors, controllers, and robotics can centralize monitoring and reduce labor but cannot replace custody and physical husbandry of the current basket.

caveats: No contract sample directly measures automation pass-through.
Independent ownership, integrator-owned pigs, and specialized phase contracts allocate costs and benefits differently.
q.change_evidence: Production and marketing contracts, technology and data ownership clauses, payment grids, feed and utility responsibility, performance bonuses, renewal history, packer formulas, basis and futures pricing, and farm margins before and after automation.
q.rationale: Independent producers can retain near-term feed, mortality, weight, throughput, and labor gains when hog prices are market-driven. Production-contract growers may retain avoided labor or utility costs but integrators can specify technology, reset fees, capture feed and animal-performance gains, or favor efficient growers at renewal; processors and formula pricing also transmit savings through competition.

caveats: Research demonstrations are not industry-wide adoption evidence.
Integrators may own data and prescribe technology, leaving contract growers little implementation discretion.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments of computer vision, acoustic or vibration monitoring, automated weighing, feed optimization, climate control, health and farrowing alerts, mortality prediction, compliance records, and scheduling, measuring caretaker hours, feed conversion, mortality, pigs saved, treatment, downtime, false alerts, and payback.
rho.rationale: Commercial barns already use environmental and feeding controls, making sensor analytics, anomaly alerts, targeted checks, forecasts, records, and administration plausible within five years. Dirty and corrosive environments, sensor maintenance, false alarms, disease biosecurity, animal-welfare validation, vendor integration, rural connectivity, facility age, and limited grower control under integrator contracts constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: The 2022 Census of Agriculture reports an average age of 58.1 for all US producers, not hog-farm ownership transfers. I translate the aged agricultural owner base into succession pressure, then discount family succession, land or barn sales without operations, contract reassignment, closures, and integrator consolidation; concentrated commercial hog farms may have more corporate ownership than the national farm population, biasing this proxy upward.
caveats: National producer age is not hog-specific and does not measure completed transfers.
Multiple family producers and corporate or integrator ownership weaken the age-to-control bridge.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession data from pork producer associations, Farm Credit, agricultural lenders, integrators, brokers, and Cooperative Extension, plus completed operating-farm deals matched to an eligible denominator and screened for control and contract continuity.
s5.rationale: Barns, permits, land, labor, and production contracts can transfer, but integrator consent, disease history, environmental liabilities, facility condition, debt, family ownership, and single-customer dependence can prevent a qualifying sale.

principal_driver: Dense recurring animal, feed, health, and environmental data can support targeted care and operational decisions across standardized production barns.
principal_weakness: Integrator control and commodity or contract pricing limit firm eligibility and value retention, while the screen lacks both core dataset denominators.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments raising hogs and pigs, including breeding, farrowing, weanling pigs, feeder pigs, market hogs, and farrow-to-finish and finishing operations.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 112210 Hog and Pig Farming

sources.supports: Describes a USDA-funded precision-livestock project using detection, tracking, posture, identity, body-condition, activity, and feeder-interaction measurements, grounding monitorable tasks without claiming commercial adoption.
sources.title: Automated Swine Phenotyping for Management and Research

sources.supports: Reports USDA research using seismic sensors and predictive modeling to identify sow and piglet activities and direct timely care, illustrating potential labor targeting and the need for human intervention.
sources.title: Sensors monitor farrowing activity and piglet care

sources.supports: Reports that farms with hogs declined more than 70 percent since 1990 as operations grew, phase specialization and production contracts increased, and scale and technology drove productivity, grounding consolidation and business-model risks.
sources.title: Hogs and Pork: Sector at a Glance

sources.supports: Reports 75.8 million hogs and pigs, 11.92 pigs saved per litter, and that hogs owned by operations above 5,000 head but raised by contractees accounted for 50 percent of US inventory, grounding scale and contracting.
sources.title: Quarterly Hogs and Pigs, December 2024

sources.supports: Reports an average US producer age of 58.1 in 2022, up 0.6 years from 2017, used as a broad succession-pressure proxy rather than hog-specific transfer evidence.
sources.title: USDA releases 2022 Census of Agriculture data

sources.supports: Forecasts 2026 pork production at 27.995 billion pounds, 1.5 percent above 2025, and 2027 production at 28.290 billion pounds, 1.1 percent above 2026, while projecting lower hog prices.
sources.title: Hogs and Pork: Market Outlook

sources.supports: Projects US pork exports to increase 8.6 percent from 7.2 billion pounds in 2026 to 7.8 billion in 2035, supported by higher weights, pigs per litter, inventories, and foreign demand.
sources.title: U.S. pork exports projected to grow, remain higher than broiler chicken and beef exports

title: Hog and Pig Farming
